name: RabbitMQ
scope: A person with BASIC proficiency level in RabbitMQ as a competency should be able to explain what RabbitMQ is, the problems it solves in distributed systems, and its core messaging concepts (producer, consumer, broker, message, queue, exchange, binding, routing key, vhost, acknowledgement). They should understand standard exchange types (direct, fanout, topic, headers), routing key usage, durable vs transient queues and messages, and basic message structure (body, headers, properties). They can use at least one mainstream client library (Java, .NET, Python, Node.js, Spring AMQP) to connect, declare exchanges/queues, publish/consume messages, and configure basic QoS (prefetch). They understand manual vs auto-acknowledgement, basic dead-lettering, message TTL, and at-least-once delivery semantics. They can design simple topologies (work queues, pub/sub, routing, dead-letter queues), reason about consumer scaling and basic flow control, and recognize ordering limitations with multiple consumers. They know how to run RabbitMQ locally (package, binary, container), enable the management UI, create/manage vhosts, users, and permissions, and configure basic server settings. They understand basic security (auth, permissions, TLS need), multi-tenancy, and management UI usage for monitoring metrics, troubleshooting, and inspecting entities. They can perform basic operational tasks (start/stop/restart, cleanup unused entities, export/import definitions), recognize resource alarms, and escalate advanced issues. They can write integration tests with local/containerized RabbitMQ, validate routing/ack flows, and understand the need for contract/schema validation. They document naming conventions, message formats, and participate in discussions on message flows and topology, collaborating with other teams and escalating complex issues appropriately.
long_scope:
{"sections":[{"name":"Core RabbitMQ & Messaging Fundamentals","scope_included":"- Understands RabbitMQ as a message broker implementing the AMQP 0-9-1 protocol and what problems it solves in distributed systems
- Understands basic messaging concepts: producer, consumer, broker, message, queue, routing, acknowledgement
- Can explain the difference between synchronous request/response interactions and asynchronous, message-driven interactions
- Understands common use cases for RabbitMQ: work queues, background job processing, decoupling services, simple publish/subscribe, buffering traffic
- Understands the benefits of message queues: decoupling, load leveling, reliability, and resiliency to transient failures
- Can explain that RabbitMQ routes messages via exchanges and bindings rather than sending directly to queues
- Understands that messages are pushed to consumers (via subscriptions) instead of consumers polling the broker
- Recognizes that RabbitMQ is stateful, maintains queues in memory/disk, and has constraints on memory, disk, and file descriptors
- Understands the concepts of channels vs TCP connections and why multiple channels per connection are used","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Core Entities: Exchanges, Queues, Bindings, Routing Keys","scope_included":"- Understands the purpose and role of each AMQP entity: exchange, queue, binding, routing key, virtual host
- Knows the standard exchange types: direct, fanout, topic, headers, and can describe when each is appropriate at a basic level
- Understands how routing keys are matched for direct and topic exchanges and how fanout disregards routing keys
- Understands the difference between the default exchange and user-defined exchanges, and when the default exchange is typically used
- Can design simple topologies for typical patterns: work queue, broadcast, routing by simple categories, basic pub/sub
- Understands auto-delete and exclusive queue semantics and when they are typically used
- Understands the difference between durable queues, transient queues, and auto-generated (server-named) queues
- Understands that queues are bound to exactly one virtual host and that exchanges and queues do not cross vhost boundaries
- Knows how to reason about routing: what happens to a message when
  - there is no matching binding
  - there are multiple bindings
  - the exchange does not exist
- Understands that bindings are one-way relationships from an exchange to a queue
- Understands how dead-letter exchanges and dead-letter queues conceptually fit into the routing model, even if not deeply optimizing their configuration","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Message Structure, Durability, and Basic Reliability","scope_included":"- Understands the basic structure of a message: body, properties (headers, content-type, correlation-id, reply-to, message-id, timestamp, expiration, delivery-mode), and delivery metadata
- Understands transient vs persistent messages (delivery-mode) and how they interact with durable queues for basic durability
- Understands that durability does not guarantee zero data loss, but greatly reduces loss under normal broker restarts
- Understands consumer acknowledgements (manual vs auto-ack) and that manual acknowledgements are required for reliable processing
- Understands the broker’s behavior on ack, nack, reject, and requeue, including the risk of message redelivery loops if misused
- Understands that RabbitMQ provides at-least-once delivery semantics by default, and that duplicates are possible
- Has a basic conceptual understanding of idempotent message processing and why it is important with at-least-once delivery
- Understands message time-to-live (TTL) concepts at queue and message level and their effect on message expiry
- Understands the basic concepts of dead-lettering for failed or expired messages and how they can support retry and parking-lot patterns
- Understands that very large messages or very large numbers of unacknowledged messages have implications for memory and disk usage
- Knows that message ordering is per-queue and per-consumer and that multiple consumers affect effective ordering guarantees","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Application Integration & Client Library Usage","scope_included":"- Can use at least one mainstream RabbitMQ client library (such as Java amqp-client, Spring AMQP, .NET client, Python client, or Node.js client) to connect, publish, and consume messages
- Understands the role of a connection factory or equivalent configuration object in the chosen client library
- Can set up a connection, open a channel, declare an exchange and queue (idempotently), and create bindings from application code
- Can implement a simple producer that publishes messages with appropriate routing keys and durability settings
- Can implement a simple consumer using callbacks, listeners, or polling interfaces provided by the client library
- Understands the difference between auto-acknowledge and manual acknowledge in client configuration and chooses correctly for reliability
- Understands how to configure basic QoS (prefetch) from the client side to control the number of unacknowledged messages per consumer
- Understands how to set basic connection properties such as heartbeat, connection timeout, and automatic recovery if the library supports it
- Can use correlation-id and reply-to properties to implement basic RPC-style interactions over RabbitMQ where required
- Understands the consequences of using long-lived vs short-lived connections and channels and follows the recommended pattern of few shared connections and multiple channels
- Understands how to handle basic connection and channel errors in the client code and retry or re-establish connections when needed","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Basic Topology & Pattern Design","scope_included":"- Can design and explain simple work queue topologies with multiple competing consumers for load distribution
- Can design simple publish/subscribe topologies using fanout exchanges with multiple independent queues bound for different consumers
- Can design basic routing topologies with direct or topic exchanges for partitioning messages by simple keys (such as type or category)
- Understands when to create separate queues per consumer group vs sharing a queue across a consumer group
- Understands basic trade-offs between using one exchange and many routing keys, vs multiple exchanges
- Understands when temporary, auto-delete queues are appropriate for short-lived consumers
- Can reason about which fields belong in the message body vs in headers vs as routing keys for simple use cases
- Can design a basic dead-letter queue topology for handling failed messages, including separate queues for errors vs retries when appropriate at a basic level
- Understands that long-running or CPU-heavy tasks should generally be processed asynchronously via queues instead of inside synchronous request flows
- Understands the basic concept of back-pressure through queue buildup and consumer scaling, and how to react to growing queue lengths (by adding consumers or changing processing behavior)","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Delivery, Ordering, and Flow Control Basics","scope_included":"- Understands that RabbitMQ dispatches messages to consumers attached to a queue, and that multiple consumers on the same queue form a competing consumer pattern
- Understands at a basic level how prefetch (QoS) affects message distribution and consumer fairness
- Understands that message ordering is not strictly guaranteed when there are multiple consumers or when messages are requeued or dead-lettered
- Understands that consumer processing time and prefetch size can cause uneven load distribution across consumers
- Understands the implications of long-running message handlers on throughput and resource usage
- Can configure simple flow control via prefetch settings to prevent a consumer from being overwhelmed by too many in-flight messages
- Understands the effect of client-side batching (multiple unacked deliveries) vs per-message ack on throughput and reliability at a basic level
- Understands that server-side memory and disk alarms can pause publishing when thresholds are exceeded, and recognizes this as a sign of flow control problems in the system
- Understands that slow or blocked consumers can cause queues to grow, which has direct resource implications on the broker","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Security and Multi-Tenancy Basics","scope_included":"- Understands the purpose of virtual hosts (vhosts) for separating logical environments and applications
- Understands the basics of user authentication using username/password and the concept of RabbitMQ users
- Understands basic authorization concepts in RabbitMQ: permissions for configure, write, and read on vhosts, exchanges, and queues
- Can reason about which users and permissions are appropriate for producers, consumers, and administrators in simple environments
- Understands the need for secure transport (TLS) and basic implications, even if not fully configuring advanced TLS settings independently
- Understands the risks of using default credentials and the need to change them in any non-trivial environment
- Understands that access to the management UI and monitoring endpoints should be restricted to authorized personnel
- Understands basic multi-tenant concerns: keeping unrelated applications in separate vhosts, avoiding accidental cross-application interference, and isolating access via user permissions","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Monitoring, Observability & Basic Troubleshooting","scope_included":"- Understands how to interpret key metrics visible in the management UI: number of messages ready, unacknowledged, total messages, consumers, and message rates (publish, deliver, ack)
- Can use the management UI to inspect exchanges, queues, bindings, connections, channels, and consumers for basic operational insight
- Understands the significance of large backlogs of ready messages, large numbers of unacknowledged messages, and high message rates
- Can identify common misconfigurations using the UI, such as a queue not bound to the expected exchange, incorrect routing keys, or missing consumers
- Understands the basics of RabbitMQ logs: where they are located and the types of issues that appear there (connection failures, authentication errors, resource alarms)
- Can troubleshoot simple connection issues from clients: wrong host/port, wrong vhost, bad credentials, or blocked connections due to permissions
- Understands basic use of health checks and status endpoints where available, to determine if a RabbitMQ node is running and responsive
- Can recognize symptoms of misused auto-ack, such as lost messages or apparent processing successes without corresponding acknowledgements
- Understands the basic impact of consumer exceptions on message redelivery and can use ack/nack handling in client code to avoid infinite redelivery loops
- Understands how to use simple message tracing or test queues to verify that routing logic is correct","is_non_core_section":false},{"name":"Setup, Installation & Basic Configuration","scope_included":"- Can run RabbitMQ in a local development environment using common methods such as package manager, binary distribution, or container runtime
- Understands how to enable and access the management plugin for monitoring and administrative tasks
- Can configure basic server settings at a simple level via configuration file or environment variables (such as default vhost, default user, port, and basic limits)
- Can create and manage vhosts, users, and permissions through the management UI or CLI at a basic level
- Understands basic disk and memory limit concepts at configuration level, even if not deeply optimizing them
- Can import and export broker definitions (exchanges, queues, bindings, policies) for simple environments
- Understands the need for consistent configuration across environments (development, staging, production) and basic strategies to achieve this (versioned configuration, infrastructure-as-code tools)
- Understands, at a conceptual level, that clustering, mirrored queues, and high availability features exist, even if they are not designing complex topologies independently
- Can set up a single-node RabbitMQ instance appropriate for development and simple testing scenarios","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Operations, Maintenance & Basic Health Management","scope_included":"- Understands the lifecycle of a RabbitMQ node: starting, stopping, restarting the service, and the effects on connected clients
- Understands the impact of broker restarts on durable vs non-durable queues and messages and on auto-delete entities
- Understands, at a basic level, memory and disk alarm conditions in RabbitMQ and their effect on publishers and consumers
- Can use the management UI or CLI to identify queues and connections that are candidates for cleanup in a non-disruptive manner
- Understands the need for periodic housekeeping: removing unused queues, exchanges, and bindings to keep the system clean
- Understands simple backup and restore strategies for configuration (via definitions export/import) and their limitations for message data
- Understands the basic role of monitoring tools and plugins (such as metrics exporters) to send RabbitMQ metrics to external monitoring systems
- Understands when to escalate to more experienced team members for advanced operational issues such as cluster failures, disk recovery, or severe performance degradation","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Testing Strategies & Quality Practices for RabbitMQ Integrations","scope_included":"- Understands the need to test message-driven components using integration tests that exercise actual RabbitMQ interactions
- Can set up and use a local or containerized RabbitMQ instance as part of a test environment
- Understands how to write tests that verify correct routing, queue declarations, and bindings for key message flows
- Understands how to validate that consumers correctly ack, nack, and requeue messages under both success and failure conditions
- Understands that contract testing or schema validation for message payloads is important to avoid producer/consumer mismatches
- Understands the role of test queues, test exchanges, and test vhosts in safely isolating test workloads from production workloads
- Understands the limitations of mocking RabbitMQ clients and when real broker-based tests are more appropriate
- Understands that load and soak testing may be necessary for higher-throughput use cases and when to involve more experienced team members in designing such tests","is_non_core_section":true},{"name":"Collaboration, Documentation & Governance for Messaging Solutions","scope_included":"- Documents queue, exchange, and routing key naming conventions and follows team-agreed standards
- Can read and contribute to architecture diagrams that include RabbitMQ components, flows, and dependencies
- Documents message formats, required headers, and expected behaviors (ack, retry, dead-lettering) for producers and consumers
- Collaborates with backend, frontend, DevOps, and QA teams to align on message contracts, routing rules, and operational expectations
- Raises concerns when seeing anti-patterns such as overloading a single queue with many unrelated message types, or mixing test and production workloads
- Participates in design discussions about new message flows and can provide input on simple topology decisions
- Keeps environment-specific RabbitMQ configuration (vhosts, policies, users) aligned with team conventions and documents differences where necessary
- Understands the need for observability and logging from applications that integrate with RabbitMQ and advocates for including correlation-ids and trace identifiers in messages
- Escalates complex topology, performance, or reliability design decisions to more experienced practitioners while still contributing informed input based on basic RabbitMQ knowledge","is_non_core_section":true}]}
